title: The strange ethics of borrowed authority
summary: Examine the position of people who, lacking authority of their own, speak with the borrowed authority of an institution — and argue that this borrowed standing carries obligations the borrower rarely notices.
topic: What we owe when we speak with authority that is not ours
prompt: Write a 600–800-word essay on the ethics of speaking with borrowed institutional authority — as an employee, a representative, a junior official. Argue that this borrowed standing carries specific obligations distinct from those of personal speech. Use inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'Granted… Even so…' concession. Avoid bureaucratic prose and management-style abstractions.
hintStarters: Only rarely is the moment of speaking with institutional authority recognised by the speaker themselves as a moment that requires a different ethics…
What the official role conceals is the gap between the small person performing it and…
Granted, the employee who refuses to take responsibility for institutional decisions cannot be expected to bear them as if they were personal. Even so,…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự phân tán quyền tác động
sự bất đối xứng về vị thế
tính phi cá nhân chuyên nghiệp
quy tắc đã được áp dụng
trọng lượng đạo đức của chức vụ
tính có thể chịu trách nhiệm

sampleText:
Only rarely is the moment of speaking with institutional authority recognised by the speaker themselves as a moment that requires a different ethics. The customs officer who refuses a traveller's documents, the housing officer who tells a Buenos Aires tenant the application will not be reconsidered, the call-centre worker who informs a Mumbai customer that the policy is final, the new junior at a Tbilisi clinic who tells a patient the consultant cannot see them today — each, in those moments, speaks with a standing none of them possesses on their own. It is the institution that refuses, not them. They are only the voice the institution happens to be using.

This is not, in most cases, a position the speaker chose with full awareness. The new official quickly discovers that the role does most of the speaking; the words coming out of their mouth are heard not as their words but as the institution's, and the petitioner standing in front of them adjusts accordingly. There is something steadying in this. The young officer who would, on their own, struggle to refuse anyone anything can refuse with confidence in the role, because the refusal is not, strictly, theirs. The institutional voice carries the weight that the personal voice could not.

What the official role conceals is the gap between the small person performing it and the much larger authority their words borrow. The customs officer is not the customs service. The housing officer is not the housing department. The call-centre worker is not the policy. The petitioner does not always see this gap — and sometimes the speaker stops seeing it themselves. What begins as a professional distancing (the rule, not me) can curdle, over years, into a deeper detachment in which the person inside the role no longer registers what the role is doing to the people who appear before it. The moral cover that helps the young official function on their first day becomes, by the tenth year, something else.

It is the gradual confusion of the borrowed authority with one's own, not the borrowing itself, that produces the small cruelties characteristic of institutional life. To say 'I am sorry; the rules require this' is one kind of speech — it preserves the gap between the person and the role, and acknowledges the petitioner as someone to whom the speaker, in their personal capacity, would have replied differently. To say 'no' without that acknowledgement, in the impersonal voice of pure procedure, is a different speech act. The decision may be the same; the relation it constitutes is not. The first leaves the petitioner with their dignity. The second treats the petitioner as a procedure-processing problem, even when no rule required that treatment.

Granted, the employee who refuses to take responsibility for institutional decisions cannot be expected to bear them as if they were personal; nor can they constantly apologise for outcomes they did not design and cannot change. The teacher who must enforce an examinations policy they do not endorse, the nurse who must follow protocols they would have written differently, the customs officer who must apply visa rules made in another office — none of these can be asked to carry the full moral weight of institutional decisions as if they were their own. To do so would crush them. Even so, the existence of that real limit does not abolish the smaller obligation. The smaller obligation is: to speak as the role without disappearing into the role, to preserve, in tone and bearing, the small acknowledgement that there is a person inside who, were they not bound by the office, would have approached the case differently.

There is also a quieter responsibility in the other direction. The petitioner who recognises the borrowed character of the authority before them has a different stance to take than the petitioner who confuses the speaker with the institution. To rage at the housing officer for the housing crisis is to mistake the borrower for the lender; the housing officer is a small person carrying decisions made far above them. None of this excuses cruelty performed in an institutional voice. It only locates responsibility where it actually sits — not entirely with the small person performing the role, and not entirely with the abstract institution that designed it, but distributed across both, with the small obligations falling to the person who happens to be the voice that day.

None of this argues for less authority in institutional life. Institutions need authority, and the people who carry it need the protection that the role's standing provides. The argument is narrower: that the borrowing creates a small ethical asymmetry the borrower carries even after the working day ends. To speak with authority one has not earned is, briefly, to owe the person spoken to a courtesy that exceeds the role — the small gesture of remaining, inside the office one is performing, also a person. Without that gesture, what the institution does in one's voice is also, somewhere, what one has done.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: It is the institution that refuses, not them. They are only the voice the institution happens to be using

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the words coming out of their mouth are heard not as their words but as the institution's

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the petitioner standing in front of them adjusts accordingly

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the role does most of the speaking

sampleAnnotations.phrase: What the official role conceals is the gap between the small person performing it and the much larger authority their words borrow
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Những gì vai trò chính thức che giấu là khoảng cách giữa người nhỏ bé thực hiện nó và uy quyền lớn hơn nhiều mà lời nói của họ vay mượn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The moral cover that helps the young official function on their first day becomes, by the tenth year, something else

sampleAnnotations.phrase: It is the gradual confusion of the borrowed authority with one's own, not the borrowing itself, that produces the small cruelties characteristic of institutional life

sampleAnnotations.phrase: To say 'I am sorry; the rules require this' is one kind of speech — it preserves the gap between the person and the role

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion after 'Only rarely' with a passive construction
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu mở 'Only rarely is the moment of speaking with institutional authority recognised by the speaker themselves as a moment that requires a different ethics' đảo trợ động từ 'is' (bị động 'is recognised') trước chủ ngữ dài 'the moment of speaking with institutional authority'. Đảo ngữ này đặt ngay đầu bài quan sát rằng đạo đức của khoảnh khắc đó hiếm khi được người nói nhận ra.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Only rarely is the moment of speaking with institutional authority recognised by the speaker themselves as a moment that requires a different ethics' inverts the passive 'is' before a long subject. The inversion sets up the central neglect: that the moment carries distinct ethical weight is rarely registered by the speaker.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to locate the source of institutional cruelty
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is the gradual confusion of the borrowed authority with one's own, not the borrowing itself, that produces the small cruelties characteristic of institutional life' dùng cleft 'It is X, not Y, that Z' để chuyển trọng tâm chẩn đoán: vấn đề không phải là việc vay mượn uy quyền (cần thiết và bình thường) mà là việc dần nhầm lẫn nó với uy quyền của chính mình.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the gradual confusion of the borrowed authority with one's own, not the borrowing itself, that produces the small cruelties characteristic of institutional life' uses 'It is X, not Y, that Z' to relocate the source of institutional cruelty: not the borrowing as such, but the slow conflation of borrowed with personal authority.

sampleTranslationVi:
Chỉ hiếm khi khoảnh khắc nói với uy quyền thể chế được chính người nói nhận ra như một khoảnh khắc yêu cầu một đạo đức khác. Nhân viên hải quan từ chối giấy tờ của một du khách, nhân viên nhà ở nói với người thuê ở Buenos Aires rằng đơn xin sẽ không được xem xét lại, nhân viên tổng đài thông báo cho khách hàng Mumbai rằng chính sách là cuối cùng, người mới ở phòng khám Tbilisi nói với bệnh nhân rằng bác sĩ tư vấn không thể gặp họ hôm nay — mỗi người, trong những khoảnh khắc đó, nói với một vị thế mà không ai trong họ sở hữu riêng. Chính thể chế từ chối, không phải họ. Họ chỉ là tiếng nói mà thể chế tình cờ đang sử dụng.

Đây không, trong hầu hết các trường hợp, là một vị trí người nói chọn với nhận thức đầy đủ. Viên chức mới nhanh chóng phát hiện ra rằng vai trò làm hầu hết việc nói; các từ ra khỏi miệng họ được nghe không phải là từ của họ mà là của thể chế. Có điều gì đó vững vàng trong điều này. Viên chức trẻ, một mình, khó từ chối ai bất cứ điều gì có thể từ chối với sự tự tin trong vai trò. Tiếng nói thể chế mang trọng lượng mà tiếng nói cá nhân không thể.

Những gì vai trò chính thức che giấu là khoảng cách giữa người nhỏ bé thực hiện nó và uy quyền lớn hơn nhiều mà lời nói của họ vay mượn. Nhân viên hải quan không phải là cơ quan hải quan. Nhân viên nhà ở không phải là sở nhà ở. Nhân viên tổng đài không phải là chính sách. Người đến xin không phải lúc nào cũng thấy khoảng cách này — và đôi khi chính người nói ngừng thấy nó. Những gì bắt đầu như sự cách ly chuyên nghiệp (quy tắc, không phải tôi) có thể đông lại, qua nhiều năm, thành sự tách rời sâu hơn trong đó người bên trong vai trò không còn ghi nhận những gì vai trò đang làm với những người xuất hiện trước nó.

Chính sự nhầm lẫn dần dần uy quyền vay mượn với uy quyền của chính mình, không phải sự vay mượn tự thân, mới tạo ra những sự tàn nhẫn nhỏ đặc trưng của đời sống thể chế. Nói 'Tôi xin lỗi; các quy tắc yêu cầu điều này' là một loại lời nói — nó bảo toàn khoảng cách giữa người và vai trò, và thừa nhận người đến xin như ai đó mà người nói, trong tư cách cá nhân, sẽ trả lời khác đi. Nói 'không' mà không có sự thừa nhận đó, bằng giọng phi cá nhân của thủ tục thuần tuý, là một hành động lời nói khác. Quyết định có thể giống nhau; mối quan hệ nó tạo ra thì không.

Cứ cho là nhân viên từ chối chịu trách nhiệm cho các quyết định thể chế không thể được mong đợi gánh chúng như thể chúng là cá nhân; cũng không thể họ liên tục xin lỗi cho các kết quả họ không thiết kế và không thể thay đổi. Giáo viên phải thực thi chính sách kiểm tra mà họ không ủng hộ, y tá phải tuân theo các giao thức mà họ sẽ viết khác đi, nhân viên hải quan phải áp dụng các quy tắc thị thực được làm ở văn phòng khác — không ai trong số này có thể được yêu cầu mang theo trọng lượng đạo đức đầy đủ của các quyết định thể chế như thể chúng là của họ. Làm vậy sẽ đè nát họ. Dù vậy, sự tồn tại của giới hạn thực sự đó không xoá bỏ nghĩa vụ nhỏ hơn. Nghĩa vụ nhỏ hơn là: nói nhân danh vai trò mà không biến mất vào vai trò.

Cũng có một trách nhiệm trầm lặng hơn theo hướng khác. Người đến xin nhận ra đặc tính vay mượn của uy quyền trước mặt mình có một lập trường khác để có so với người đến xin nhầm người nói với thể chế. Phẫn nộ với nhân viên nhà ở vì khủng hoảng nhà ở là nhầm người vay với người cho vay; nhân viên nhà ở là một người nhỏ bé mang theo các quyết định được làm xa hơn nhiều phía trên họ. Không có gì trong số này biện minh cho sự tàn nhẫn được thực hiện bằng giọng thể chế. Nó chỉ đặt trách nhiệm ở nơi nó thực sự nằm — không hoàn toàn với người nhỏ bé thực hiện vai trò, và không hoàn toàn với thể chế trừu tượng đã thiết kế nó.

Không có gì ở đây tranh luận cho ít uy quyền hơn trong đời sống thể chế. Các thể chế cần uy quyền, và những người mang nó cần sự bảo vệ mà vị thế của vai trò cung cấp. Lập luận hẹp hơn: rằng sự vay mượn tạo ra một sự bất đối xứng đạo đức nhỏ mà người vay mang theo ngay cả sau khi ngày làm việc kết thúc. Nói với uy quyền mà mình chưa kiếm được là, ngắn gọn, nợ người được nói tới một sự lịch thiệp vượt quá vai trò — cử chỉ nhỏ của việc vẫn còn, bên trong chức vụ mình đang thực hiện, cũng là một con người. Không có cử chỉ đó, những gì thể chế làm trong giọng nói của mình cũng, ở đâu đó, là những gì mình đã làm.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly applies inversion after 'Only rarely' with passive construction?
options: Only rarely the moment of speaking with institutional authority is recognised.
Only rarely is the moment of speaking with institutional authority recognised by the speaker themselves as a moment that requires a different ethics.
The moment only rarely is recognised by speakers.
Only rarely recognised is the moment of speaking with institutional authority.
mcQuestions.explanation: After 'Only rarely', the passive 'is' inverts before the long subject 'the moment of speaking with institutional authority'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central observation about institutional speech?
options: The speaker speaks with their own personal authority
The speaker has no authority at all
The speaker speaks with a standing they do not possess on their own — they are the voice the institution happens to be using
The speaker always disagrees with the institution
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's opening insight: the speaker's words are heard as the institution's, and they themselves are merely the voice in that moment — not the source of the authority.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the opening paragraph for the borrowed-voice argument.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay say the role is initially 'steadying'?
options: Because it makes the official rich
Because it makes everyone friendly
Because it removes all responsibility
Because the role's authority allows a young officer to refuse with confidence they could not muster on their own — the refusal is not, strictly, theirs
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay observes that institutional authority is a real support: it allows people to do hard things (refuse, decide) that would be psychologically difficult to do as themselves.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the second paragraph for the steadying effect.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft to locate the source of institutional cruelty?
options: It is the gradual confusion of the borrowed authority with one's own, not the borrowing itself, that produces the small cruelties characteristic of institutional life.
The borrowing itself produces institutional cruelty.
Both borrowing and confusion produce institutional cruelty.
Institutional cruelty has no identifiable source.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, not Y, that Z' is a cleft that distinguishes the necessary practice (borrowing) from the corrupting one (confusion of borrowed with personal authority).

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the moral difference between the two refusals the essay describes?
options: Only the words used differ

'I am sorry; the rules require this' preserves the gap between person and role and treats the petitioner as a person, while 'no' in impersonal procedure-voice treats them as a procedure-processing problem — even when the same decision is being made
The decisions are different in each case
The first is illegal
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's key distinction: the decision may be identical; the relation the speech act constitutes is not. Tone and acknowledgement matter morally.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the central paragraph about the two ways to say no.

options: Even so, the employee bears all responsibility. Granted, they bear none.
Granted and even so — the employee is both responsible and not.

Granted, the employee who refuses to take responsibility for institutional decisions cannot be expected to bear them as if they were personal. Even so, the existence of that real limit does not abolish the smaller obligation — to speak as the role without disappearing into the role.
Granted that responsibility is personal. Even so, it is collective.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted' concedes the real limit on personal responsibility; 'Even so' insists this does not abolish the smaller obligation: preserving the gap between person and role.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say about the petitioner who rages at the housing officer?
options: They are absolutely right to do so
They should be punished for their rage
They are correctly assigning institutional blame
They mistake the borrower for the lender — the housing officer is a small person carrying decisions made far above them; rage at the wrong location of responsibility
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's symmetric move: the petitioner also has an obligation to distinguish the speaker from the institution. Misdirected rage is itself a moral error.

mcQuestions.prompt: Where does the essay say responsibility actually sits?
options: Distributed across both — with the small obligations falling to the person who happens to be the voice that day
Entirely with the small official
Entirely with the abstract institution
Nowhere — institutional speech is unaccountable
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay refuses both extremes: responsibility is distributed; the small person bears the small obligations (tone, acknowledgement); the institution bears the larger ones (design, decisions).
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the relational paragraph for the distribution argument.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the 'smaller obligation' the essay names?
options: To resign from any role one disagrees with

To speak as the role without disappearing into the role — to preserve, in tone and bearing, the small acknowledgement that there is a person inside who, were they not bound by the office, would have approached the case differently
To take full personal blame for institutional decisions
To apologise constantly
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's positive prescription: stay visible as a person inside the role. Don't disappear into the impersonal voice of pure procedure.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the Granted/Even so paragraph for the smaller obligation.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say happens to professional distancing over years?
options: It always remains a healthy boundary
It becomes friendlier
It can curdle, over years, into a deeper detachment in which the person inside the role no longer registers what the role is doing to the people before it
It disappears entirely
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's longitudinal observation: useful distancing on day one becomes deadened detachment by year ten — the moral cover hardens into moral blindness.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the third paragraph for the longitudinal point.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say borrowed authority does for the officer who is afraid to refuse?
options: Nothing — they remain afraid
It makes them more aggressive
It causes them to refuse incorrectly
It allows them to refuse with confidence in the role, because the refusal is not, strictly, theirs — institutional authority carries the weight personal authority cannot
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay acknowledges the genuine support borrowed authority provides: it allows action that the personal self could not perform.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay say borrowed authority creates that 'one carries even after the working day ends'?
options: A small ethical asymmetry — the borrower owes the person spoken to a courtesy that exceeds the role, the gesture of remaining, inside the office, also a person
Nothing — borrowed authority disappears at clock-out
A formal legal liability
A psychological depression
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's closing claim: the borrowing does not end when work ends. The asymmetry persists, and so does the small obligation to have spoken without disappearing into the role.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph for the closing claim.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's view on whether institutions need authority?
options: Institutions should be abolished
Institutions need authority; the people who carry it need the protection it provides — the argument is not for less authority but for awareness of the borrowing's ethical asymmetry
Authority is always evil
Authority should be distributed equally
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay explicitly disclaims an anti-institutional reading: 'None of this argues for less authority in institutional life.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph's opening disclaimer.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'moral cover' mean as the essay uses it?
options: A formal apology
An insurance policy

The psychological permission the role provides — letting the small person do hard things (refuse, decide) without bearing the full personal weight — useful initially but liable to deepen into detachment
A religious ceremony
mcQuestions.explanation: Moral cover: the role's authority shields the personal self from full burden. Useful for functioning; dangerous if it becomes a permanent retreat.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the third paragraph for moral cover.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay's final line imply?
options: The institution alone bears the consequences of its actions
Speaking in an institutional voice has no personal consequences
Personal ethics and institutional ethics are completely separate
What the institution does in one's voice is also, somewhere, what one has done — the borrowing carries personal moral weight that cannot be fully shed by appealing to the role
mcQuestions.explanation: The closing line: 'what the institution does in one's voice is also, somewhere, what one has done.' The borrowing creates a residual personal liability — small, but real.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a moment when you spoke with borrowed institutional authority — at work, in a role, as a representative. Did you preserve the small gap between yourself and the role, or did you collapse into the impersonal procedure-voice? On the essay's terms, what small acknowledgement could you have offered the person in front of you, and what would it have cost you to offer it?
